perturbation.original_text: Seller covenants and agrees that Seller will not, without NIM's prior written consent, directly or indirectly, either on behalf of himself or on behalf of any business venture, as an employee, consultant, partner, principal, stockholder, officer, director, trustee, agent, or otherwise (other than on behalf of NIM or its Affiliates): <PAGE> (A) Engage in the business of providing record-keeping and administrative services for retirement plans and sales of insurance products to clients of third party administrators (the "Restricted Business") anywhere in the Territory (as defined below), or be employed by, engage or participate in the ownership, management, operation or control of, or act in any advisory, expert, consulting or other capacity for, any entity or individual engaged in the Restricted Business anywhere in the geographical area within the United States (the "Territory");
perturbation.changed_text: Seller covenants and agrees that Seller will endeavor not to, absent unreasonable denial of NIM's consent, directly or indirectly, either on behalf of himself or on behalf of any business venture, as an employee, consultant, partner, principal, stockholder, officer, director, trustee, agent, or otherwise (other than on behalf of NIM or its Affiliates): <PAGE> (A) Participate more than negligibly in the business of possibly providing record-keeping and administrative services for retirement plans and sales of insurance products to clients of third party administrators (the "Restricted Business") in a manner that NIM might deem competitive, or be nominally employed by, engage somewhat in the ownership, passive management, loose operation or light control of, or act tangentially in an advisory, expert, consulting or other capacity for, any entity or individual engaged in a vaguely similar business anywhere in certain areas within the United States (the "Territory");
perturbation.explanation: The original text had clear restrictions on the seller's activity within the restricted business. The modified text introduces several ambiguities: "endeavor not to," "absent unreasonable denial," "participate more than negligibly," "possibly providing," "might deem competitive," "nominally employed," "somewhat in the ownership," "passive management," "loose operation," "light control," "tangentially in an advisory," "vaguely similar business," and "certain areas." These phrases significantly weaken the restrictions and make them difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Sherman Antitrust Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. While non-compete agreements can be permissible under certain circumstances (e.g., as part of a business sale), the ambiguity introduced could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade if the vague restrictions are used to stifle legitimate competition without clear and justifiable business reasons. The numerous subjective terms undermine the clarity needed to assess whether the non-compete is narrowly tailored and reasonable, potentially leading to an unlawful restraint.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 1 : Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 1-MONOPOLIES AND COMBINATIONS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Short Title Executive Documents §1. Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1 - Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty U.S. Code Notes prev | next Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be deemed guilty of a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding $100,000,000 if a corporation, or, if any other person, $1,000,000, or by imprisonment not exceeding 10 years, or by both said punishments, in the discretion of the court. (July 2, 1890, ch. 647, § 1, 26 Stat. 209 ; Aug. 17, 1937, ch. 690, title VIII, 50 Stat. 693 ; July 7, 1955, ch. 281, 69 Stat. 282 ; Pub. L. 93–528, § 3 , Dec. 21, 1974 , 88 Stat. 1708 ; Pub. L. 94–145, § 2 , Dec. 12, 1975 , 89 Stat. 801 ; Pub. L. 101–588, § 4(a) , Nov. 16, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2880 ; Pub. L. 108–237, title II, § 215(a) , June 22, 2004 , 118 Stat. 668 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2004— Pub. L. 108–237 substituted “$100,000,000” for “$10,000,000”, “$1,000,000” for “$350,000”, and “10” for “three”. 1990— Pub. L. 101–588 substituted “$10,000,000” for “one million dollars” and “$350,000” for “one hundred thousand dollars”. 1975— Pub. L. 94–145 struck out from first sentence two provisos granting anti-trust exemption to State fair trade laws. 1974— Pub. L. 93–528 substituted “a felony, and, on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding one million dollars if a corporation, or, if any other person, one hundred thousand dollars, or by imprisonment not exceeding three years” for “a misdemeanor, and on conviction thereof, shall be punished by fine not exceeding f
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces numerous ambiguities that significantly weaken the original restrictions on the seller's activity, making the non-compete agreement difficult to enforce. The original text had clear restrictions, but the changed text uses terms such as "endeavor not to," "absent unreasonable denial," "participate more than negligibly," and "possibly providing." These weaken the non-compete agreement, which could be construed as an unreasonable restraint of trade. This violates the Sherman Antitrust Act, as reflected in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, which state: 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal.' The ambiguities undermine the clarity needed to assess if the non-compete is narrowly tailored and reasonable, potentially leading to an unlawful restraint.

perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that following the termination of Seller's employment or consulting period with NIM or any Affiliate of NIM and for all times thereafter, he shall keep secret all such Confidential Information and that he will not, directly or indirectly, use for his own benefit or for the benefit of others nor Disclose (as hereinafter defined), without the prior written consent of NIM, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that following the termination of Seller's employment or consulting period with NIM or any Affiliate of NIM and for a period he subjectively deems reasonable thereafter, he should attempt to keep mostly secret arguably Confidential Information and that he may, within certain ethical boundaries, use for his perceived benefit or for the general benefit of others and possibly Disclose (as hereinafter defined), without any unreasonably withheld consent of NIM, some Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the seller will keep secret all Confidential Information forever. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating "for a period he subjectively deems reasonable," "should attempt to keep mostly secret," "arguably Confidential Information," "perceived benefit," "general benefit of others," "possibly Disclose," "unreasonably withheld consent," and "some Confidential Information." These phrases make the obligation to protect confidential information vague and subjective, undermining the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by State - No Federal Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no federal law, many states recognize the inevitable disclosure doctrine, which prevents a former employee from working for a competitor if their new job duties will inevitably lead them to disclose the former employer's trade secrets. The modification, by making the obligation to keep information secret subjective and discretionary, directly weakens the protection of confidential information that the inevitable disclosure doctrine is intended to provide. The ambiguous language suggests a weaker standard of protection than courts would typically apply when considering inevitable disclosure claims.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates perpetual secrecy of confidential information, while the modified version introduces subjectivity and discretion, significantly weakening this obligation. The LLM-generated 'Law Explanation' highlights that the modification weakens the protection of confidential information that the inevitable disclosure doctrine is intended to provide. The scraped snippets do not contain relevant information. The changes introduce terms like 'subjectively deems reasonable,' 'should attempt to keep mostly secret,' and 'arguably Confidential Information,' all of which undermine the clear and enforceable nature of the original non-disclosure agreement.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the restrictive covenants contained in Sections 1, 2 and/or 3 of this Agreement shall be found by a court of competent jurisdiction to be unreasonable by reason of such restrictive covenants extending for too great a period of time or over too great a geographic area or by reason of such restrictive covenants being too extensive in any other respect, then such restrictive covenant shall be deemed modified to the minimum extent necessary to make such restrictive covenant reasonable and enforceable under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: In the possible event that the restrictive covenants contained in Sections 1, 2 and/or 3 of this Agreement are somehow viewed by some court to perhaps be unreasonable for potentially subjective reasons relating to the somewhat questionable scope of such covenants, then such restrictive covenant might be slightly adjusted to a degree possibly required to arguably render such restrictive covenant more palatable and enforceable, maybe.
perturbation.explanation: The original text outlines a clear 'blue pencil' doctrine. The modified text weakens it with phrases like "possible event," "somehow viewed," "some court," "perhaps be unreasonable," "potentially subjective reasons," "somewhat questionable scope," "might be slightly adjusted," "degree possibly required," "arguably render such restrictive covenant more palatable," and "maybe." This language makes the blue pencil doctrine uncertain and less effective.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184 discusses unenforceable contracts due to public policy and the divisibility of terms. The original clause aims to ensure enforceability by allowing a court to modify unreasonable restrictions. By weakening the blue pencil doctrine with ambiguous language, the modified text decreases the likelihood that a court will effectively modify the contract to make it enforceable, potentially resulting in the entire covenant being struck down, which could be against public policy promoting reasonable enforcement of contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces significant ambiguity and uncertainty into the 'blue pencil' doctrine, potentially hindering a court's ability to modify unreasonable restrictions and enforce the contract. This undermines the original clause's intent to ensure enforceability. The LLM's Law Explanation states the change decreases the likelihood that a court will modify the contract to make it enforceable, potentially resulting in the entire covenant being struck down, which could be against public policy promoting reasonable enforcement of contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referring to § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause, states that the court may 'so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result.' The modifications weaken the mechanism for the court to do so, risking the clause being deemed unenforceable in its entirety.

perturbation.original_text: If any action for breach of or to enforce the provisions of this Agreement is commenced, the court in such action shall award to the party in whose favor a judgment is entered, a reasonable sum as attorneys' fees and costs. Such attorneys' fees and costs shall be paid by the non-prevailing party in such action.
perturbation.changed_text: If any action for potential breach of or to perhaps enforce some of the provisions of this Agreement is possibly commenced, the court in such action may, at its discretion, maybe award to a party it feels is more deserving, a somewhat fair sum as attorneys' fees and questionable costs. Such attorneys' fees and costs might be paid by a party the court deems less sympathetic in such action.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear mandate for awarding attorneys' fees and costs to the prevailing party. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating "potential breach," "perhaps enforce some of the provisions," "possibly commenced," "may, at its discretion," "maybe award," "a party it feels is more deserving," "somewhat fair sum," "questionable costs," and "might be paid by a party the court deems less sympathetic." These phrases transform a mandatory obligation into a discretionary one, dependent on the court's subjective assessment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 8101
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 8101 generally entitles the prevailing party in a civil action to recover costs. While the statute allows for some judicial discretion in awarding costs, the original contract language aims to create a more certain right to attorneys' fees and costs for the prevailing party. The modified language undermines this by making the award discretionary and introducing subjective elements, potentially conflicting with the general principle of awarding costs to the prevailing party under New York law.

Costs in an action. The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts NY CPLR § 8101. The original text mandates awarding attorney's fees and costs to the prevailing party. The modified text introduces discretion, stating the court "may" award fees to a party it "feels is more deserving," and the costs are "questionable." This conflicts with § 8101, which states "the party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances." The modified text makes the award discretionary and subjective, undermining the entitlement to costs established by law. The original contract aligns with the legal entitlement, while the modified version subverts it, thus creating a contradiction.
